name: Fête de l'Ours (Bear Festival)
description: Ancient pre-Christian festival celebrating the awakening of the bear from hibernation and the symbolic return of spring. A man dressed in animal skins and covered in black grease acts as 'the bear' and is pursued through the village streets by 'hunters' dressed in white. The bear tries to catch and smear young women with black grease. Eventually the bear is captured, 'shaved' by mock barbers in a ritual cleansing ceremony, and then escapes again. Arles-sur-Tech is the oldest continuous location of the Vallespir Bear Festival tradition. The festival is deeply rooted in Catalan folk tradition and is considered one of the oldest carnival-related rites in Europe.
tradition_type: Bear Festival / Pre-Christian Ritual / Catalan Carnival

name: Fête de l'Ours (Bear Festival)
description: One of the three main Vallespir Bear Festival locations. In Prats-de-Mollo, the festival features a particularly elaborate version: the bear (a man in shaggy brown costume) is chased through the streets by hunters, eventually captured, and subjected to a comedic 'shaving' ceremony by mock barbers in the town square. The bear is then paraded before the crowd. The festival incorporates music, dancing, and communal feasting. Prats-de-Mollo's version is known for the theatrical elaborateness of the bear-hunt sequence.
tradition_type: Bear Festival / Pre-Christian Ritual / Catalan Carnival

name: Fête de l'Ours (Bear Festival)
description: Third location of the traditional Vallespir Bear Festival network, in the town of Saint-Laurent-de-Cerdans in the Pyrénées-Orientales. Shares the core bear-hunt symbolism with the Arles-sur-Tech and Prats-de-Mollo festivals: the awakening and pursuit of the bear as a symbol of winter being chased away by spring. The three Vallespir municipalities collectively preserve this UNESCO-nominated Catalan carnival tradition.
tradition_type: Bear Festival / Pre-Christian Ritual / Catalan Carnival
